reasoning: All three nail the load-bearing errors: post-hoc causation, revenue-per-employee as an invalid productivity proxy (with the headcount arithmetic), cherry-picked competitors, and the unjustified leap to a full five-day mandate. C edges ahead by being the most rigorous and best-calibrated: it explicitly enumerates the five causal stories a correlation permits (including reverse causation and the firm going remote BECAUSE it was struggling), names the headcount denominator effect concretely, flags the hard-to-vary/unfalsifiable structure, and ends with an explicit probability (5 to 10%) plus a concrete action plan and 2019-vs-2020 baseline correction. B is nearly as strong, with crisp fallacy labels (begging the question on 'clearly', false dichotomy, single-cause) and a good cost-benefit section, but lacks the explicit calibration C provides. A is solid and clear, opens well by flagging the cumulative-vs-annual ambiguity (~2% per year) and stresses the counterfactual, but it is less structured, names fewer fallacies explicitly, and never surfaces reverse causation or selection-denominator framing as sharply, so it lands third.
